company.name: Cigna
company.description: Cigna provides health insurance, pharmacy benefit management, care coordination, and related health services through Cigna Healthcare and Evernorth Health Services.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: The Cigna Group is a global health company organized around Cigna Healthcare, its health benefits business, and Evernorth Health Services, its pharmacy benefit management, specialty pharmacy, care, and health-services platform.

The company reports more than 185 million customer relationships across more than 30 markets and jurisdictions, making it one of the largest scaled coordinators of U.S. health benefits and pharmacy services.

overview.title: Financial Position
paragraphs: Cigna reported roughly $274.9 billion of 2025 revenue and about $6.0 billion of net income, with revenue heavily influenced by Evernorth's pharmacy and health-services scale.
As of May 22, 2026, market-data providers placed Cigna's market capitalization near $75.7 billion, keeping it within the upper-middle tier of the S&P 500 by market value.

moatNarrative: Cigna's moat comes from employer and government-plan relationships, provider-network contracting, claims operations, pharmacy benefit scale, specialty pharmacy assets, regulatory expertise, and the switching costs embedded in large health-benefit programs.

The same complexity that frustrates buyers and patients also protects incumbents: plan administration, drug formularies, prior authorization, compliance, rebate contracting, and care coordination all require trust, data, and institutional process depth.
decentralizationNarrative: The company is difficult to decentralize directly because insurance pooling, regulated claims payment, pharmacy benefit management, and provider contracting depend on licensed entities and large risk pools.

The more realistic pressure points are modular: open health records, interoperable prior authorization APIs, transparent formulary and rebate mechanisms, cooperative purchasing groups, and patient-controlled data layers can reduce dependence on closed payer and PBM platforms without replacing insurance risk bearing overnight.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 126-150.

moat.rationale: Cigna combines large employer-plan relationships, regulated claims infrastructure, provider networks, PBM scale through Evernorth, and specialty pharmacy capabilities, all of which create high switching costs and operational barriers.

decentralizability.rationale: Core insurance risk pooling and claims payment are heavily regulated and scale-dependent, but data exchange, prior authorization, care coordination, and pharmacy purchasing workflows can be partly opened through standards and cooperative models.

profitability.rationale: Cigna is consistently profitable at large scale, with roughly $6.0 billion of 2025 net income, but health insurance and PBM operations remain relatively low-margin compared with software or branded consumer platforms.

peRatio.rationale: Market-data snapshots in late May 2026 placed Cigna's trailing P/E ratio near 12, reflecting a lower multiple than high-growth technology businesses.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported The Cigna Group market capitalization at about $75.72 billion as of May 22, 2026.

products.name: Cigna Healthcare
products.summary: Cigna Healthcare provides medical, dental, behavioral, pharmacy-adjacent, and employer health benefit products and services for individuals, employers, and organizations.
products.whyItMatters: It is the consumer- and employer-facing benefits layer where plan design, provider networks, claims handling, prior authorization, and care navigation shape access to care.
replacementSketch: A realistic open replacement would not start by recreating a national insurer. It would begin as an interoperable benefits-administration stack for cooperatives, self-funded employers, public plans, and regional provider groups.

Open records, transparent claims rules, member-owned plan governance, and portable prior-authorization workflows could make health-benefit administration less dependent on one vertically integrated carrier.

alternatives.name: OpenMRS
alternatives.summary: OpenMRS is an open-source electronic medical record platform used globally by implementers, care organizations, and public-health programs.

openness.rationale: OpenMRS is explicitly open source, community developed, and designed to avoid license fees and vendor lock-in.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It supports local implementation and adaptation across many facilities and countries, although it is clinical infrastructure rather than a complete payer replacement.

readiness.rationale: OpenMRS reports thousands of facilities, many countries, and a large community, indicating practical deployment maturity.

costLeverage.rationale: No license fees and local customization can reduce software dependence, though deployment, hosting, training, and integration still require investment.

alternatives.name: GNU Health
alternatives.summary: GNU Health is a free/libre health and hospital information system with EMR, hospital management, lab, personal health record, and federation components.

openness.rationale: GNU Health is a Free/Libre GNU project with source-code distribution and a software-freedom mission.

decentralizationFit.rationale: GNU Health includes federation-oriented components and can run locally, including embedded deployments, making it aligned with distributed health infrastructure.

readiness.rationale: GNU Health is a mature project, but readiness varies by institution because health-system deployment and integration remain demanding.

costLeverage.rationale: Free/libre licensing can reduce vendor dependency and license costs, while implementation and support costs remain material.

disruptionConcepts.name: Member-Owned Health Benefits Cooperative
disruptionConcepts.summary: A cooperative benefits administrator could let employers, unions, freelancers, and regional care groups pool administration while using open health-data standards for eligibility, claims status, prior authorization, and care-navigation workflows.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure changes if plan administration becomes a shared cooperative service with portable rules and data, reducing dependence on a single vertically integrated insurer for every operational layer.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated governance and portable health-data exchange rather than through Bitcoin payments; members coordinate rules, audits, and vendor selection without ceding the entire stack to one carrier.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Employers, member groups, administrators, clinicians, and third-party administrators coordinate through cooperative governance, shared claims and prior-authorization APIs, and auditable service-level contracts.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Claims rules, prior-authorization status, plan documents, appeal outcomes, and administrative fees would be published to members and audited by independent fiduciaries; cheating is constrained by member voting, regulator oversight, and portable data exports.
failureModes: Insurance regulation and reserve requirements may still force the cooperative to partner with licensed carriers or reinsurers.
Provider-network contracting scale may remain weaker than incumbent national insurers.
Bad governance could reproduce opaque payer behavior under a cooperative label.
adoptionPath: Start with self-funded employer groups or union plans that already separate risk funding from third-party administration.
Adopt CMS-aligned prior authorization and payer-to-payer APIs for portability and auditability.
Expand into regional cooperative purchasing and eventually licensed risk-bearing entities where regulation allows.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept moves governance and data portability away from a single carrier, but regulated insurance risk still limits full decentralization.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Self-funded plans, standards-based APIs, and health-information exchange provide a credible coordination base, but provider contracts and regulatory compliance remain hard.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open clinical systems and interoperability rules exist, but building a compliant payer-administration cooperative would require capital, actuarial expertise, regulatory work, and operational trust.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The model could pressure administrative transparency for self-funded groups, but it would not immediately match Cigna's national network, PBM, and claims scale.

products.name: Evernorth
products.category: Pharmacy benefit management and health services
products.summary: Evernorth Health Services includes pharmacy benefit management, specialty pharmacy, care management, and other health-services capabilities within The Cigna Group.
products.whyItMatters: Evernorth is the part of Cigna most exposed to concerns about PBM opacity, formulary power, rebate incentives, pharmacy access, and vertical integration in the drug supply chain.
replacementSketch: A replacement would likely begin as a transparent drug-purchasing, formulary, and claims-routing layer for employers, unions, public purchasers, and independent pharmacies.

The strongest open model would combine published formulary logic, auditable rebate or net-price handling, standards-based claims data, and cooperative purchasing governance rather than trying to replicate a closed PBM outright.

alternatives.name: OpenMRS
alternatives.summary: OpenMRS provides open clinical-record infrastructure that can support medication, encounter, and patient-data workflows adjacent to pharmacy and care-management services.

openness.rationale: OpenMRS is open source and community maintained with no license-fee model.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It can decentralize clinical data infrastructure but does not directly replace PBM contracting, rebate negotiation, or specialty pharmacy logistics.

readiness.rationale: The project reports broad real-world usage, but pharmacy benefit workflows would require additional integration.

costLeverage.rationale: Open clinical infrastructure can reduce software costs and data lock-in, but PBM savings depend on drug purchasing and pharmacy network economics.

alternatives.name: GNU Health
alternatives.summary: GNU Health's free/libre health information stack, federation components, and personal health record tools can support open care and medication-data infrastructure.

openness.rationale: GNU Health is a Free/Libre GNU project with source-code availability.

decentralizationFit.rationale: GNU Health Federation and local deployment options align with distributed health-data coordination, though not with PBM contracting by themselves.

readiness.rationale: The software is mature, but adapting it to PBM-adjacent purchasing and benefit-management workflows would require significant domain-specific work.

costLeverage.rationale: Free/libre deployment can reduce data and software lock-in, while drug pricing and specialty pharmacy costs remain separate economic problems.

disruptionConcepts.name: Transparent Cooperative PBM Clearinghouse
disruptionConcepts.summary: A cooperative PBM clearinghouse could publish formulary criteria, pass through net prices and rebates, route claims through open APIs, and give employers, unions, public purchasers, and independent pharmacies shared governance over purchasing rules.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept attacks the PBM moat by separating drug-purchasing coordination and claims routing from opaque vertically integrated rebate economics.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through cooperative governance, transparent rule publication, and multi-party audit trails; Bitcoin is not central unless later used for payment settlement or bond-based anti-fraud incentives.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Purchasers pool demand, pharmacies publish fulfillment terms, manufacturers or wholesalers submit net-price offers, and the cooperative clearinghouse applies member-approved formulary and reimbursement rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Net-price schedules, rebate receipts, spread-pricing bans, formulary exceptions, and pharmacy reimbursements would be logged for member audit; false fulfillment is constrained by pharmacy licensure, claims reconciliation, inventory records, and independent audit rights.
failureModes: Large manufacturers may refuse attractive pricing without incumbent PBM volume.
A cooperative clearinghouse could still become opaque if audit rights and member governance are weak.
Regulatory, fiduciary, HIPAA, ERISA, and state pharmacy-benefit laws could make rollout slow and jurisdiction-specific.
adoptionPath: Begin with transparent pass-through PBM services for self-funded employers and union plans.
Add independent pharmacy participation with published reimbursement logic and claims reconciliation.
Use purchasing scale and audited savings to attract public plans or regional coalitions.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model distributes governance and audit rights across purchasers and pharmacies, but still depends on regulated intermediaries and negotiated drug supply.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The FTC's PBM scrutiny identifies a clear problem and pass-through PBM models already exist conceptually, but coalition formation and manufacturer participation are hard.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Claims routing, audits, and formularies are technically feasible, but compliance, contracting, drug supply, and capital requirements make implementation difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: A credible transparent cooperative PBM could pressure pricing and rebate opacity for self-funded purchasers, but Evernorth's scale and specialty pharmacy assets remain substantial advantages.
